cluster_name: Cyclospora outbreak linked to Taylor Farms lettuce at Taco Bell
event_summary: A US cyclosporiasis outbreak that began May 1 spread across 34 states; investigators identified Taylor Farms shredded iceberg lettuce supplied to Taco Bell as the likely source, prompting Taco Bell to remove the ingredient indefinitely and the FDA to open an investigation.
tldr: Taco Bell's global brand recognition drove international pickup of a domestic US food-safety crisis that carries no cross-border disease risk. Spanish-language outlets in Mexico and Colombia led with 'diarrea explosiva' in headlines, foregrounding consumer alarm over the supply-chain or regulatory angles dominant in English coverage. The Globe and Mail's full-body extract surfaces a detail absent from most international items: the CDC has established a 'likely link' across Michigan, Ohio, West Virginia, and Kentucky, pointing to a single-source contamination event at the supplier level. India sent two outlets (Hindustan Times, Times of India) to cover the story—unusual depth from a country with no domestic exposure—further confirming that Taco Bell brand recognition, not public health proximity, determines newsworthiness globally.

evidence.quote:
Taco Bell removes lettuce from menu in US after links to explosive diarrhoea

Lettuce from one supplier is being removed indefinitely "out of an abundance of caution", the company says.

The outbreak, which began on May 1, has been concentrated in Michigan, with Ohio and New York also reporting a large number of infections. Michigan health officials on Thursday reported 4,312 cases of the parasitic illness.

Taco Bell lettuce linked to cyclospora outbreak in US; over 4300 cases reported

Michigan has reported the largest number of cases overall, with more than 4,300 illnesses and 102 hospitalizations.

frames.sub_frame: FDA investigation and precautionary lettuce removal

Taco Bell removing lettuce from some stores amid cyclosporiasis scare

Taco Bell announced on Thursday that it would remove lettuce from some of its stores following reports that the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has opened an investigation into iceberg lettuce supplied to the fast-food chain as a possible source of the cyclosporiasis outbreak in the U.S. “Based on ongoing conversations with public health officials,...

The cyclospora parasite has sickened thousands of people in 34 states.

Taco Bell to stop using lettuce identified with cyclosporiasis outbreak - Reuters

evidence.quote: Taylor Farms: All about the Taco Bell lettuce supplier linked to US cyclosporiasis outbreak

EU investiga un brote de Cyclospora vinculado con lechuga servida en Taco Bell. Los casos por ciclosporiasis ya alcanzan 34 estados.

The U.S. Centers for Disease Control and Prevention said this week it had identified a likely link among cases reported in Michigan, Ohio, West Virginia and Kentucky, suggesting many of the infections may stem from a common source, according to the report.

single_outlet_findings.finding: Only outlet to report the CDC's finding of a 'likely link' among cases in Michigan, Ohio, West Virginia, and Kentucky—implying a single-source contamination event rather than distributed exposure across the 34 affected states.
Only outlet to profile Taylor Farms as a corporate entity, identifying it as one of the largest fresh-cut vegetable producers in the US, headquartered in Salinas, California.

Only US-domestic outlet in the corpus; provides the only direct attribution of Taco Bell's public response to the FDA investigation opening, quoting corporate language about 'ongoing conversations with public health officials'.

Ran two separate pieces—one on the FDA preparing to warn consumers in 5 states, one on Taco Bell halting lettuce use—tracking the regulatory and corporate tracks independently rather than combining them as most outlets did.

Cited 1,645 CDC-confirmed cases alongside 5,100+ cases under investigation as of May 1—figures not present in any other corpus item—suggesting access to an earlier investigative dataset preceding the Michigan surge.

Framed the story around the lettuce supplier rather than Taco Bell, headlining 'major fast food and grocery lettuce supplier'—the only outlet to foreground that Taylor Farms supplies grocery retail as well as fast food.

bottom_line: A US food-safety crisis involving a parasitic outbreak across 34 states received international coverage driven by Taco Bell's brand recognition rather than any cross-border public health risk. The key unreported fact—that the CDC has established a likely common source linking four Midwestern states, pointing to a single contamination event—appeared in only one outlet's full-body extract, while most international headlines led with symptom-shock language.
